1: Chapter 27 is an appendix to Leviticus, addressing the redemption of vowed persons and property. It may have been added after the formal colophon of 26:46 because vow-law required its own framework. The chapter deals with situations where persons or property have been dedicated (neder, vowed) to YHWH and then need to be redeemed through monetary payment. The system assumes a basic principle: anything dedicated to YHWH has a cash-equivalent value that allows it to be reclaimed through payment.
2: The special vow (neder) involving the valuation of persons (be'erkekha nefashot laYHWH). A person might vow themselves or a family member to YHWH's service — probably for sanctuary service under the Levites. The valuation system allows the person to remain at home by paying a redemption price. The standardized price table (vv. 3-7) reflects an attempt to create a fair, predictable system across different economic classes and demographics.
3: The standard valuation table for vowed persons: male 20-60 = 50 shekels, the highest valuation. This is the peak productive-labor period for males in an agricultural society. The valuations are not statements about human worth but about labor-market value — the economic contribution a person in that demographic category would typically make. The sanctuary shekel (v. 25) was the standard unit: 50 shekels was a substantial sum — roughly equivalent to a year's wages for a skilled laborer in the ancient Near East.
4: Female 20-60 = 30 shekels. The lower female valuation reflects the lower market value of female labor in the ancient Israelite economy, not a theological statement about women's worth. The text offers no theological rationale; it simply reflects contemporary economic realities. Numbers 3 and 4 describe women serving at the tabernacle in various capacities, indicating that women could be valuably dedicated to sanctuary service.
5: Youth (5-20 years): male 20 shekels, female 10 shekels. The lower valuations for the youth category reflect both lower labor productivity and longer remaining dependency period. A 10-year-old male contributes less to an agricultural household's immediate productivity than a 30-year-old male, hence the lower redemption value.
6: Infancy (1 month to 5 years): male 5 shekels, female 3 shekels. The lowest valuations — infants and small children are economically dependent, not contributors. The inclusion of infants in the vow-valuation system suggests that parents did occasionally vow infants to YHWH's service (as Hannah vowed Samuel, 1 Sam 1:11) and the redemption option was available for those who later chose to reclaim the child.
7: Old age (60+): male 15 shekels, female 10 shekels. The valuations drop for those past peak productive age, reflecting declining labor capacity. Notably, older women are valued at 10 shekels (the same as young girls) while older men drop to 15 (less than half the peak male value). The elderly retain economic value in an agricultural household through accumulated experience and skilled crafts.
8: The poverty accommodation: if the vowing person cannot afford the standard valuation, the priest assesses their ability and sets a proportionate price. The priest's flexible assessment ensures the vow system is accessible to all economic levels — the wealthy pay the standard price; the poor pay what they can. This graduated approach is consistent with the graduated offering scales throughout Leviticus.
9: Vowed animals that may be offered to YHWH (clean animals from the permitted categories) become holy and cannot be redeemed — whatever is given to YHWH is absolutely his. The principle kol asher yitten mimenu laYHWH yihyeh qodesh (whatever is given to YHWH shall be holy) establishes the irrevocability of acceptable-animal vows. Unlike persons and unclean animals, a vowed sacrificial animal cannot be replaced or reclaimed.
10: The exchange prohibition: no substitution of an equivalent or different animal once vowed. If substitution is attempted, both the original and the substitute become holy — the attempted exchange backfires and costs double. This provision prevents the natural human tendency to upgrade vowed animals (promising a fat animal, delivering a lean one) or to reclaim an animal by substituting a less valuable one.
11: Vowed unclean animals (those not eligible for sacrifice) are assessed by the priest — they cannot be offered but can be redeemed for their monetary value. The unclean animal vow creates an anomaly: the animal cannot go to the altar but the person has dedicated it. The priest's valuation provides the mechanism for converting the vow into a cash equivalent that can be applied to the sanctuary.
12: The priest's assessment is authoritative — ka'asher yaarekh hakohen ken yihyeh (as the priest values it, so it shall be). The priest functions as an objective third-party appraiser, ensuring the assessed value reflects market reality rather than the vow-maker's self-interest. The priestly appraisal system is the ancient world's equivalent of independent professional valuation.
13: The redemption option for vowed unclean animals: pay the assessed value plus a fifth (ḥomesh). The 20% surcharge on redemptions appears throughout ch. 27 (vv. 13, 15, 19, 27, 31) as a consistent redemption fee. The surcharge ensures that redemption costs more than the original commitment — it prevents the system from being used to temporarily park assets with YHWH and then reclaim them at no cost.
14: A vowed house is assessed by the priest as good or bad quality — its value is objectively determined, not self-reported. The house-vow system allowed a person to donate real property to the sanctuary while retaining the option to redeem it. Archaeological evidence shows Israelite houses varying enormously in size and construction quality from a single room to multi-room compounds — the priest's quality assessment was necessary to set a fair value.
15: Redemption of a vowed house: the assessed value plus the one-fifth surcharge. The right of redemption makes the vow system flexible — vows are not irrevocable gifts but formal commitments with a built-in buy-back option at a premium. This flexibility encouraged generosity (people could vow without fear of permanent loss) while the surcharge maintained the seriousness of the commitment.
16: Vowed inherited field (ancestral land): the valuation is based on its seed capacity — how much grain it produces per cycle, measured in homer of barley seed. A homer of barley seed was equivalent to roughly 220 liters — the valuation is 50 shekels per homer's worth of seed capacity. This seed-based valuation system tied the field's worth to its agricultural productivity rather than arbitrary market price.
17: If dedicated in a jubilee year, the field's full value applies — the entire productive cycle from jubilee to jubilee remains. This is the maximum value: the field will produce crops for the full 49-year cycle before reverting to its original owner at the next jubilee. The jubilee system intersects directly with the vow system — the field's value is always calculated relative to the remaining jubilee cycle.
18: If dedicated after the jubilee, the priest calculates the proportionate remaining years — the value decreases as fewer productive years remain before the next jubilee. The pro-rated valuation is a sophisticated actuarial calculation: the field's value in the vow system declines as the jubilee approaches, exactly as the field's market value would decline in a system where all land reverts at jubilee.
19: Redemption of a vowed field: assessed value plus the fifth. The surcharge applies consistently to all redemptions throughout ch. 27. If the person who vowed the field wants it back, they pay more than its assessed value — the 20% premium is the cost of reneging on the commitment.
20: If the vowed field is not redeemed or has been sold to another person, it may no longer be redeemed. The irrevocability kicks in when the field changes hands — once sold by the sanctuary to a third party, the original vow-maker's redemption right is extinguished. The sanctuary's property rights are protected against an original owner trying to reclaim through a third-party sale.
21: At jubilee, the unredeemed vowed field becomes ḥerem to YHWH and passes to the priests. Ḥerem (devoted/under the ban) is the term for something irrevocably belonging to YHWH that cannot be redeemed or sold — the opposite of the standard vow with its redemption option. The field's jubilee status as priestly inheritance means it becomes permanently attached to the Levitical landholding system.
22: Purchased (non-ancestral) field: a person can also vow a field they bought rather than inherited. The purchased field has a different legal status than the ancestral field — it reverts to the original owner at jubilee anyway, not to the vow-maker. The vow-valuation for a purchased field is calculated for the jubilee remainder only, since the field's permanent ownership is already determined.
23: The priest calculates the assessed value to the jubilee year; the vow-maker pays the assessed amount as holy to YHWH on that day. The immediate payment (bayom hahu, on that day) prevents deferred payment that might become default. The sanctuary receives the cash value of the purchased field's remaining years rather than the field itself, which will revert to its original landowner at jubilee.
24: The jubilee reversion of the purchased field to the original owner confirms that the purchased field was never truly the vow-maker's to give permanently. The jubilee principle — land ultimately belongs to YHWH and only to Israel as tenants (Lev 25:23) — constrains what can be permanently donated to the sanctuary.
25: All valuations use the sanctuary shekel (sheqel haqqodesh): 20 gerahs to the shekel. The sanctuary shekel was the standard unit of weight for all cultic transactions — it may have been a slightly heavier shekel than the commercial shekel, ensuring the sanctuary received full weight. Standardized weights were a persistent problem in the ancient world; the sanctuary shekel represents an attempt to create a reliable standard for sacred transactions. Archaeological finds of stamped weights from the Iron Age confirm weight standardization was practiced in Israel.
26: Firstborn animals are excluded from the vow system — they already belong to YHWH (Exod 13:2; 34:19-20). You cannot vow what you do not own. The firstborn principle is one of the oldest covenant obligations in the Torah: the first product of the womb belongs to YHWH, whether animal or human (human firstborns are redeemed). The exclusion from vow-making reflects the prior claim YHWH has on all firstborns.
27: Unclean firstborns (those from species not eligible for sacrifice) may be redeemed at the assessed value plus the fifth — or if not redeemed, sold at the assessed value. The unclean firstborn cannot go to the altar but must still be handled through the sanctuary's economic system. The redemption plus surcharge maintains the consistent 20% premium across all redemption contexts.
28: akh-kol-ḥerem asher yaḥarim ish laYHWH: 'But no devoted thing (ḥerem) — anything that a man irrevocably devotes to YHWH — shall be sold or redeemed.' The ḥerem category is the absolute counterpart to the standard vow: while standard vows can be redeemed (with surcharge), ḥerem devotion is completely irrevocable. Everything devoted under ḥerem is most holy to YHWH and cannot return to human use. This is the principle applied to Jericho (Josh 6:17-19) and to the destruction of Canaanite nations (Deut 7:2, 20:16-17).
29: Ḥerem applied to persons: any person irrevocably devoted (ḥerem) cannot be ransomed — he shall surely be put to death (mot yumat). This is the most severe application of the ḥerem principle: what belongs to YHWH absolutely cannot be reclaimed. The contexts for human ḥerem in the OT are primarily warfare (captive enemies devoted to destruction) and capital offenses (Achan's violation of Jericho's ḥerem, Josh 7:15). The verse is not a mechanism for arbitrary human sacrifice but for the execution of those placed under formal covenant ban.
30: The tithe (ma'aser) — every tenth of the land's produce, seed, and fruit trees — is holy to YHWH. The tithe is described as qodesh laYHWH (holy to YHWH), establishing it as a category alongside the sanctuary shekel and the firstborn. The tithe system had multiple layers in the Torah: a Levitical tithe (Num 18:21-24), a festival tithe (Deut 14:22-27), and a poor tithe (Deut 14:28-29). Leviticus 27 establishes the base principle that a tenth belongs to YHWH.
31: Tithe redemption: a person may redeem their agricultural tithe by paying its assessed value plus the fifth surcharge. The 20% premium applies here as throughout ch. 27. The redemption option for the agricultural tithe allowed households to convert the tithe from produce to cash — practical for those distant from the sanctuary or with perishable surplus.
32: Livestock tithe: every tenth animal that passes under the shepherd's rod. The method — counting animals sequentially and dedicating every tenth one regardless of quality — prevents the tithe-payer from selecting the worst animals for YHWH. The rod-counting method (also attested in later rabbinic practice) removes human judgment from the selection: the tenth one is the tenth one, whether healthy or lame.
33: No quality-selection for the livestock tithe — the counted tenth is given regardless. If a substitution is attempted (exchanging a better for a worse, or worse for better), both the original tenth and the substitute become holy. The same anti-substitution rule as for vowed sacrificial animals (v. 10): attempted evasion doubles the commitment rather than reducing it.
34: The final verse of Leviticus: elleh hamitsvot asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe el-bnei Yisrael behar Sinai: 'These are the commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for Israel on Mount Sinai.' The colophon closes the entire book with the Sinai authority formula. Leviticus begins with YHWH calling to Moses from the completed tabernacle (1:1) and ends with all the commandments traced back to Sinai — the book's legislative authority rests on both the ongoing presence (tabernacle) and the founding revelation (Sinai).
